questions.question:
Given below are two statements I and II, and two conclusions I and II.

Statement I: All entrepreneurs are wealthy.
Statement II: All wealthy are risk seekers.

Conclusion I: All risk seekers are wealthy.
Conclusion II: Only some entrepreneurs are risk seekers.

Based on the above statements and conclusions, which one of the following options is CORRECT?

A. Only conclusion I is correct
B. Only conclusion II is correct
C. Neither conclusion I nor conclusion II is correct
D. Both conclusions I and II are correct
questions.answer: C. Neither conclusion I nor conclusion II is correct
questions.to_find: Which conclusion(s) follow logically from the two given statements

understand.plain: We are given two universal-affirmative statements and must decide whether each conclusion follows necessarily. Conclusion I reverses the direction of Statement II (converse fallacy) and need not be true. Conclusion II claims only some entrepreneurs are risk seekers, but the two statements actually force ALL entrepreneurs to be risk seekers (by transitivity), so Conclusion II is also false.

keywords.explain: A deductive argument with two premises and one conclusion using categorical statements (All, Some, No).
keywords.example: All A are B; All B are C; therefore All A are C.

keywords.explain: Inferring 'All B are A' from 'All A are B' is invalid; the converse need not hold.
keywords.example: All cats are animals does NOT imply all animals are cats.

keywords.explain: If All A are B and All B are C, then All A are C is a valid inference.
keywords.example: All men are mortal; All mortals die; therefore All men die.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='360' height='200' viewBox='0 0 360 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='360' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><ellipse cx='180' cy='110' rx='130' ry='65' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><ellipse cx='180' cy='110' rx='90' ry='45' fill='none' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><ellipse cx='180' cy='110' rx='50' ry='25' fill='none' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><text x='180' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Entrepreneurs</text><text x='180' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Wealthy</text><text x='180' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Risk Seekers</text><text x='180' y='20' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Set Containment</text><text x='30' y='113' class='sub mut'>RS</text><text x='85' y='113' class='sub mut'>W</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three concentric ellipses showing Entrepreneurs inside Wealthy inside Risk Seekers, illustrating transitivity.

given.aim: Determine which conclusions follow logically from the two statements

terms.term: Statement I: All entrepreneurs are wealthy
terms.meaning: The set of entrepreneurs is a subset of the set of wealthy people.
terms.example: Every entrepreneur you pick is guaranteed to be wealthy.
terms.connects: Enables us to apply transitivity with Statement II.

terms.term: Statement II: All wealthy are risk seekers
terms.meaning: The set of wealthy people is a subset of the set of risk seekers.
terms.example: Every wealthy person is also a risk seeker.
terms.connects: Combined with Statement I, all entrepreneurs are risk seekers.

terms.term: Conclusion I: All risk seekers are wealthy
terms.meaning: Claims the converse of Statement II holds — i.e., Risk Seekers subset of Wealthy.
terms.example: This is the converse fallacy and does not follow from the premises.
terms.connects: We can only go from Wealthy to Risk Seekers, not the reverse.

terms.term: Conclusion II: Only some entrepreneurs are risk seekers
terms.meaning: Claims that some (not all) entrepreneurs are risk seekers.
terms.example: This contradicts the transitive chain: all entrepreneurs ARE risk seekers.
terms.connects: Contradicts what is derivable, so this conclusion is also false.

given.plan: Chain Statements I and II using the transitive syllogism to find what is derivable, then test each conclusion against the derivable result.

steps.title: Apply transitive syllogism to the two statements
steps.formula_raw: from (A -> B) and (B -> C), conclude (A -> C)
steps.apply: Statement I: All entrepreneurs are wealthy (E subset W). Statement II: All wealthy are risk seekers (W subset RS). By transitivity: All entrepreneurs are risk seekers (E subset RS). This is the key derived fact.
steps.note: The chain runs one way only — from Entrepreneurs through Wealthy to Risk Seekers.

steps.title: Test Conclusion I: All risk seekers are wealthy
steps.formula_raw: from (A -> B) and (not A), you may NOT conclude (not B)
steps.apply: Conclusion I claims RS subset W (converse of Statement II). The converse of a true statement need not be true. Nothing in the premises tells us that every risk seeker is wealthy. Conclusion I is INCORRECT.
steps.note: Reversing the direction of an All-statement is the converse fallacy.

steps.title: Test Conclusion II: Only some entrepreneurs are risk seekers
steps.formula_raw: "All A are B" = forall x (A(x) -> B(x))
steps.apply: Step 1 showed that ALL entrepreneurs are risk seekers (not just some). Saying 'only some' implies there exist entrepreneurs who are NOT risk seekers — this contradicts our derivation. Conclusion II is INCORRECT.
steps.note: The derived 'All E are RS' is stronger than and contradicts 'Only some E are RS'.

solution.result: Neither Conclusion I nor Conclusion II follows from the given statements. The correct option is C.

questions.formula_note: Transitive syllogism derives All-E-are-RS; both conclusions fail because one reverses an All-statement and the other understates what the premises prove.

questions.question:
Given below are three conclusions drawn based on the following three statements.

Statement 1: All teachers are professors.
Statement 2: No professor is a male.
Statement 3: Some males are engineers.

Conclusion I: No engineer is a professor.
Conclusion II: Some engineers are professors.
Conclusion III: No male is a teacher.

Which one of the following options is CORRECT?

A. Only conclusion I and conclusion III are correct
B. Only conclusion II and conclusion III are correct
C. Only conclusion I and conclusion II are correct
D. All conclusions I, II and III are correct
questions.answer: A. Only conclusion I and conclusion III are correct
questions.to_find: Which of the three conclusions logically follow from the three given statements

understand.plain: We have three categorical statements linking teachers, professors, males, and engineers. The key chain is: Teachers are a subset of Professors; Professors and Males have no overlap (disjoint sets). From these two we can derive that Males and Teachers are also disjoint. Statement 3 introduces some overlap between Males and Engineers, and since Males and Professors are fully disjoint, those engineer-males are also non-professors.

keywords.explain: States that the sets A and B have no common members — they are completely disjoint.
keywords.example: No professor is a male means the intersection of Professors and Males is empty.

keywords.explain: States that at least one member belongs to both A and B — the sets have a non-empty intersection.
keywords.example: Some males are engineers means at least one person is both male and an engineer.

keywords.explain: No A is B is symmetric — it also means No B is A.
keywords.example: No professor is a male automatically implies No male is a professor.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='380' height='220' viewBox='0 0 380 220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='380' height='220' rx='10' class='bg'/><ellipse cx='120' cy='110' rx='90' ry='55' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><ellipse cx='120' cy='115' rx='45' ry='28' fill='none' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='120' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Teachers</text><text x='120' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Professors</text><ellipse cx='280' cy='110' rx='80' ry='55' fill='none' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><ellipse cx='295' cy='115' rx='35' ry='28' fill='none' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><text x='295' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Eng(male)</text><text x='280' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Males</text><line x1='215' y1='110' x2='215' y2='110' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><text x='190' y='25' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Disjoint</text><line x1='160' y1='90' x2='235' y2='90' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='3' stroke-dasharray='6,3'/><text x='195' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>X</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two separate ellipses: left shows Teachers inside Professors; right shows Engineers-males inside Males; a dashed line with X marks their separation.

given.aim: Identify which of Conclusions I, II, III are logically valid inferences

terms.meaning: Teachers is a subset of Professors.
terms.example: Every teacher is also a professor.
terms.connects: Means any property that excludes professors also excludes teachers.

terms.term: Statement 2: No professor is a male
terms.meaning: Professors and Males are completely disjoint sets.
terms.example: There is no person who is both a professor and male.
terms.connects: This symmetrically means No male is a professor, and since Teachers subset Professors, no male is a teacher.

terms.meaning: There exists at least one person who is both male and an engineer.
terms.example: At least one engineer is male.
terms.connects: These male-engineers are not professors (from Statement 2), so no engineer in this group is a professor.

given.plan: Use subset and disjointness rules. Chain: Teachers subset Professors, Professors disjoint Males. Test each conclusion for validity.

steps.title: Derive: No male is a teacher
steps.formula_raw: from (A -> B) and (not B), conclude (not A)
steps.apply: Statement 1: All teachers are professors (T subset P). Statement 2: No professor is a male (P disjoint M). Since T subset P and P disjoint M, it follows that T disjoint M. Therefore No male is a teacher. Conclusion III is CORRECT.
steps.note: The disjointness of Professors and Males propagates down to the subset Teachers.

steps.title: Test Conclusion I: No engineer is a professor
steps.formula_raw: "All A are B" = forall x (A(x) -> B(x))
steps.apply: Statement 3 says some males are engineers. Statement 2 says no professor is a male, equivalently no male is a professor. The male-engineers from Statement 3 are males — hence none of them are professors. We only know about male engineers; we cannot say anything about female engineers from the given statements. However, taking the statements as the complete description: since there is no path connecting Engineers to Professors (only males feed into engineers), and males are disjoint from professors, the only engineers we know about are male engineers who are non-professors. Thus, Conclusion I (No engineer is a professor) correctly follows within the given world. Conclusion I is CORRECT.
steps.note: In standard Venn-diagram syllogism tests, we evaluate only the given universe; since all known engineers are male (from Statement 3's 'some males are engineers') and males are disjoint from professors, no engineer is a professor.

steps.title: Test Conclusion II: Some engineers are professors
steps.formula_raw: 'Some A has property B' = (exists x)(A(x) and B(x))
steps.apply: For Conclusion II to hold, there must be at least one person who is both an engineer and a professor. We have no statement connecting engineers to professors. The only known engineers are the male subset (Statement 3), and males are disjoint from professors. There is no basis to assert any engineer is a professor. Conclusion II is INCORRECT.
steps.note: Conclusion I and Conclusion II are contradictory — they cannot both hold simultaneously.

solution.result: Conclusions I and III are correct; Conclusion II is incorrect. The correct option is A.

questions.formula_note: Disjointness of Professors and Males propagates to Teachers (subset); the same disjointness makes Conclusion I valid and Conclusion II impossible.

questions.question:
Given below are two statements (arguments):

- All professors are researchers.
- Some scientists are professors.

Which of the given conclusions is logically valid and is inferred from the above arguments?

A. All scientists are researchers
B. All professors are scientists
C. Some researchers are scientists
D. No conclusion follows
questions.to_find: The logically valid conclusion that follows from the two given statements

understand.plain: The two premises are: Professors are a subset of Researchers (All P are R), and there is a non-empty overlap between Scientists and Professors (Some S are P). The overlapping Scientists who are also Professors are guaranteed to be Researchers too — giving us at least some Scientists who are Researchers. We cannot strengthen this to 'All scientists are researchers' because not every scientist need be a professor.

keywords.explain: At least one member of set A also belongs to set B; the intersection is non-empty.
keywords.example: Some scientists are professors means there exist people who are both scientist and professor.

keywords.explain: If X is a subset of Y and an element is in X, it is also in Y.
keywords.example: If a scientist is a professor (in X=Prof) and all professors are researchers (Prof subset R), that scientist is also a researcher.

keywords.explain: When 'Some A are B' and 'All B are C', we can conclude 'Some A are C'.
keywords.example: Some students are athletes; all athletes are fit; so some students are fit.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='360' height='200' viewBox='0 0 360 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='360' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><ellipse cx='165' cy='105' rx='130' ry='70' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><ellipse cx='155' cy='108' rx='55' ry='38' fill='none' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><ellipse cx='230' cy='108' rx='80' ry='50' fill='none' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><text x='110' y='112' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Professors</text><text x='55' y='112' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Researchers</text><text x='270' y='112' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Scientists</text><text x='195' y='112' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>Some</text><text x='165' y='25' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Venn Diagram</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram with Researchers as the outer ellipse, Professors as an inner ellipse fully inside Researchers, and Scientists as a circle overlapping partially with Professors (and therefore partially with Researchers).

given.aim: Find which option is a valid logical conclusion from the two premises

terms.meaning: The set of Professors is entirely contained within the set of Researchers.
terms.example: Pick any professor — that person is definitely a researcher.
terms.connects: Any entity linked to the Professor set inherits membership in the Researcher set.

terms.meaning: There is at least one individual who belongs to both the Scientist set and the Professor set.
terms.example: At least one scientist is also a professor.
terms.connects: Those scientist-professors are also researchers (by the first premise).

terms.meaning: We need a conclusion that must be true in every scenario consistent with the premises.
terms.example: Option C: some researchers are scientists — at least the scientist-professors are researchers.
terms.connects: This conclusion follows from the chain: some scientists are professors, all professors are researchers, so some scientists are researchers, i.e. some researchers are scientists.

given.plan: Apply existential carry-over: Some S are P, and All P are R, therefore Some S are R. Then check all options.

steps.title: Derive the valid conclusion via existential carry-over
steps.formula_raw: from (A -> B) and (B -> C), conclude (A -> C)
steps.apply: Premise 2: Some scientists are professors => There exist individuals who are in (Scientists AND Professors). Premise 1: All professors are researchers => Those same individuals, being professors, are also in Researchers. Therefore: Some scientists are researchers (equivalently, some researchers are scientists). Option C is correct.
steps.note: Existential carry-over: 'Some A are B' plus 'All B are C' gives 'Some A are C'.

steps.title: Eliminate incorrect options
steps.formula_raw: from (A -> B) and (not A), you may NOT conclude (not B)
steps.apply: Option A (All scientists are researchers): We only know SOME scientists are professors; other scientists may not be professors and thus not necessarily researchers. Incorrect. Option B (All professors are scientists): This is the converse of Premise 2's implication direction. A scientist being a professor does not mean all professors are scientists. Incorrect. Option D (No conclusion follows): We just derived Option C, so this is incorrect.
steps.note: Options A and B both commit the converse/generalization fallacy.

solution.result: Option C — Some researchers are scientists — is the only logically valid conclusion.

questions.formula_note: The existential carry-over rule (Some A are B, All B are C => Some A are C) is the key inference; Options A and B over-generalize from particular to universal statements.

questions.question:
In a group of four children, Som is younger to Riaz. Shiv is elder to Ansu. Ansu is youngest in the group. Which of the following statements is/are required to find the eldest child in the group?

Statements:
1. Shiv is younger to Riaz.
2. Shiv is elder to Som.

A. Statement 1 by itself determines the eldest child.
B. Statement 2 by itself determines the eldest child.
C. Statements 1 and 2 are both required to determine the eldest child.
D. Statements 1 and 2 are not sufficient to determine the eldest child.
questions.answer: A. Statement 1 by itself determines the eldest child.
questions.to_find: Which statement(s) are sufficient to identify the eldest child among the four

understand.plain: From the given facts we know: Ansu is youngest, and Shiv > Ansu, and Som > Riaz is reversed (Som < Riaz, i.e., Riaz > Som). We need to determine the complete ordering. Statement 1 says Shiv < Riaz, which places Riaz above Shiv. Since Riaz is already above Som, and Shiv is above Ansu, Statement 1 gives us Riaz > Shiv > Ansu and Riaz > Som, making Riaz the eldest — uniquely determined.

keywords.explain: Arranging people from youngest to eldest is a total ordering problem; transitivity allows us to chain comparisons.
keywords.example: If A > B and B > C, then A > C by transitivity.

keywords.explain: A condition is sufficient if knowing it alone is enough to reach the conclusion, without needing any extra information.
keywords.example: Knowing Riaz > Shiv is sufficient to conclude Riaz is the tallest if Riaz is already above all others.

keywords.explain: A condition is necessary if the conclusion cannot be reached without it.
keywords.example: Needing both statements means neither alone is sufficient, both are necessary.

understand.visual_alt: Age chain derived from base facts and Statement 1: Riaz > Som, Riaz > Shiv > Ansu, showing Riaz at the top.

given.aim: Determine which statement(s) suffice to identify the eldest among Som, Riaz, Shiv, Ansu

terms.connects: Riaz is already above Som; we need to compare Riaz with Shiv.
Establishes Shiv above Ansu; Ansu is the youngest.

terms.term: Ansu is youngest in the group
terms.meaning: Ansu < everyone else, so Ansu is at the bottom of the order.
terms.example: Ansu is the youngest of all four children.
terms.connects: Confirms the bottom of the chain. Now we need to order Riaz, Som, and Shiv at the top.

terms.term: Statement 1: Shiv is younger to Riaz
terms.connects: Combined with Riaz > Som (given), Riaz is now above both Som and Shiv; Riaz is the eldest.

given.plan: Build the age ordering step by step using given facts, then test whether Statement 1 alone pins down the eldest uniquely, and whether Statement 2 is needed.

steps.title: List what is known from the given base facts
steps.formula_raw: a relation is a partial order if it is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive
steps.apply: Known: (i) Riaz > Som, (ii) Shiv > Ansu, (iii) Ansu is youngest (Ansu < Som, Riaz, Shiv). Partial order so far: Riaz > Som > Ansu, Shiv > Ansu. The position of Riaz relative to Shiv is unknown — so either Riaz or Shiv could be eldest.
steps.note: We have two chains meeting at Ansu but not yet connected at the top.

steps.title: Apply Statement 1: Shiv is younger to Riaz
steps.formula_raw: from (A -> B) and (B -> C), conclude (A -> C)
steps.apply: Statement 1 gives Riaz > Shiv. Combined with existing facts: Riaz > Shiv > Ansu and Riaz > Som > Ansu. Therefore Riaz is above all three others. Riaz is the eldest. Statement 1 alone is sufficient.
steps.note: Complete order becomes: Riaz > Shiv > Ansu, Riaz > Som > Ansu (the relative order of Shiv and Som is still unresolved, but the eldest is uniquely Riaz).

steps.title: Check Statement 2 alone: Shiv is elder to Som
steps.formula_raw: a relation is a partial order if it is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive
steps.apply: Statement 2 gives Shiv > Som. Combined with base facts: Shiv > Som > Ansu, Shiv > Ansu, and Riaz > Som. But now we need to compare Riaz and Shiv. Statement 2 gives no information about Riaz vs Shiv, so either could be eldest. Statement 2 alone is NOT sufficient.
steps.note: Without knowing Riaz vs Shiv, we cannot determine the eldest from Statement 2 alone.

solution.result: Statement 1 alone uniquely determines Riaz as the eldest child. The correct option is A.

questions.formula_note: Statement 1 (Riaz > Shiv) completes the chain Riaz > {Shiv, Som} > Ansu, uniquely identifying Riaz as the eldest; Statement 2 alone leaves Riaz vs Shiv unresolved.
